perturbation.original_text: If you believe that the Company has billed you incorrectly, you must contact Company no later than 60 days after the closing date on the first billing statement in which the error or problem appeared, in order to receive an adjustment or credit.
perturbation.changed_text: If you believe that the Company has billed you incorrectly, you must contact Company no later than 14 days after the closing date on the first billing statement in which the error or problem appeared, in order to receive an adjustment or credit.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time to report billing errors from 60 days to 14 days is unreasonable and potentially violates consumer protection laws, particularly those addressing fair billing practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fair Credit Billing Act (FCBA) provides consumers with specific rights and procedures for resolving billing errors. While it does not explicitly state a maximum time frame, reducing the time to report errors to 14 days severely restricts a consumer's ability to review statements and gather necessary documentation, which contradicts the FCBA's intent to protect consumers from unfair billing practices. Courts often look at 'reasonableness' in these situations.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time to report billing errors from 60 to 14 days, creating a legal contradiction. This violates the intent of consumer protection laws, as stated in the Perturbation and Law Explanations, which aim to protect consumers from unfair billing practices. The reduction severely restricts the consumer's ability to review statements, gather documentation, and respond which undermines consumer protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the expansion of the subchapter to include the protection of consumers against inaccurate and unfair credit billing and credit card practices.

perturbation.original_text: Amounts which remain unpaid for 30 days are subject to a finance charge of 1.5% per month on any outstanding balance, or the maximum permitted by law, whichever is lower, plus all expenses of collection and may result in immediate termination of Service.
perturbation.changed_text: Amounts which remain unpaid for 5 days are subject to a finance charge of 1.5% per month on any outstanding balance, or the maximum permitted by law, whichever is lower, plus all expenses of collection and may result in immediate termination of Service.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the grace period for late payments to 5 days is likely to violate state laws on late fees and consumer protection, as 5 days may not be considered a reasonable grace period.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the specific grace period is not explicitly mandated in Section 1722, requiring payment within 5 days could be viewed as an unreasonable or unfair business practice, especially concerning service agreements. It could be seen as a violation, especially if it involves automatic termination of service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1722. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1722. (a) (1) Whenever a contract is entered into between a consumer and a retailer with 25 or more employees relating to the sale of merchandise which is to be delivered by the retailer or the retailer’s agent to the consumer at a later date, and the parties have agreed that the presence of the consumer is required at the time of delivery, the retailer and the consumer shall agree, either at the time of the sale or at a later date prior to the delivery date, on a four-hour time period within which any delivery shall be made. Whenever a contract is entered into between a consumer and a retailer with 25 or more employees for service or repair of merchandise, whether or not the merchandise was sold by the retailer to the consumer, and the parties have agreed that the presence of the consumer is required at the time of service or repair, upon receipt of a request for service or repair under the contract, the retailer and the consumer shall agree, prior to the date of service or repair, on a four-hour period within which the service or repair shall be commenced. Once a del
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the grace period for late payments from 30 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation states that a 5-day grace period might violate state laws on late fees and consumer protection, potentially being considered unreasonable. The Law Explanation further supports this, suggesting that requiring payment within 5 days could be viewed as an unreasonable or unfair business practice, especially if it involves service termination. Although the provided law snippet, CIV 1722, focuses on delivery timeframes and doesn't directly address grace periods, the broader principle of fair business practices and consumer protection (implied in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation) is undermined by such a short grace period, making it an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to earlier termination as provided below, this Service Agreement is for the total duration of the Company's Offering (the "Initial Term") unless either party requests termination at least 30 days prior to the end of the then-current term.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to earlier termination as provided below, this Service Agreement is for the total duration of the Company's Offering (the "Initial Term") unless either party requests termination at least 5 days prior to the end of the then-current term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the required notice period for termination to 5 days may be deemed unreasonable, particularly given the potential disruption this could cause to both parties. This may contradict contract law principles requiring reasonable notice for contract termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Notice of Termination
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309(3) states that termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. Five days' notice may not be considered reasonable, especially in a service agreement context where winding down operations or finding alternative arrangements may require more time. The reasonableness will often depend on how dependent the parties are on the service.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 5 days, which may contradict the 'reasonable notification' requirement for contract termination as stated in UCC § 2-309(3). Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party'. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that 5 days' notice may not be considered reasonable in a service agreement, thus creating a potential contradiction.
